title: Wild Cats That Soak Their Food! Iberian Lynxes Dunk Rabbits in Water
summary:
Imagine a lion or a wolf soaking its dinner in water like you might soak beans before cooking. Sounds weird, right? Well, scientists in Spain were shocked when camera traps caught wild Iberian lynxes doing just that!

The Iberian lynx is one of the rarest wild cats in the world. It lives in Spain and Portugal and mostly eats European rabbits. Between 2020 and 2025, researchers recorded eight times when female lynxes carried dead rabbits to water troughs and dunked them. The longest soak was over four minutes!

Why would a meat-eater do this? Scientists have a couple of ideas. One is that the lynxes are trying to add water to the rabbit to help their cubs stay hydrated. Another is that soaking softens the rabbit's fur and meat, making it easier for cubs to chew when they switch from milk to solid food. Experiments showed that a soaked rabbit carcass can hold nearly 4% of its weight in water 40 minutes later.

What's even cooler is that the behavior seems to be spreading among lynxes that are related or live near each other. That means they might be learning from one another! This shows that these wild cats are smarter than we thought. It's the first time scientists have ever seen a meat-eating animal soak its prey. Pretty amazing, right?
why_it_matters: This shows that even wild cats can have special tricks to take care of their babies, and that animals are smarter than we often think.

keywords.explanation: The Iberian lynx is a rare wild cat that lives in Spain and Portugal.
Camera traps are cameras that automatically take pictures when an animal moves in front of them.
Water troughs are containers that hold drinking water for animals.
A carcass is the dead body of an animal, especially one used for food.
Prey is an animal that another animal hunts and eats.

questions.question: What animal did scientists observe soaking its food?
Where did the lynxes soak the rabbits?
How long was the longest soak?
Why might the lynxes soak the rabbits?
What happened when scientists soaked a rabbit carcass 40 minutes later?
Why is this behavior interesting to scientists?
options: Because it shows lynxes are smarter than thought
questions.correct_answer: Because it shows lynxes are smarter than thought

background_read: The Iberian lynx is a very rare wild cat.
Only a few hundred are left in the wild, living in the forests of Spain and Portugal.
Article_Structure: WHO: Scientists in Spain studying Iberian lynxes.
WHAT: Observed female lynxes soaking dead rabbits in water troughs.
WHERE: In Spain and Portugal, at water troughs used by animals.
WHY: Probably to help keep their cubs hydrated and soften the rabbit meat.

perspectives.description: They are very surprised by this behavior and think it shows intelligent problem-solving in lynxes.
They are happy to learn more about the habits of this endangered species, which can help protect them.
People find it fascinating that such a rare animal does something so unusual, making them more interested in wildlife.
